era_context: Early Bronze Age III in the Levant; Early Dynastic Period in Mesopotamia; Indus Valley Civilisation in its mature phase; Old Kingdom Egypt (Dynasty 5–6 transition). This is a period of established urban civilisations, long-distance trade, and competitive city-state politics in Mesopotamia.

events.title: Lagash–Umma conflict (attested period)
events.description: The city-states of Lagash and Umma engage in ongoing territorial disputes over control of the Guedena plain and irrigation rights. These conflicts are attested in multiple inscriptions from rulers of both cities across the Early Dynastic period. The disputes reflect the fundamental competition for water resources in southern Mesopotamia.
key_figures: Rulers of Lagash and Umma (specific names uncertain for this year)

sources.name: Inscriptions from Lagash and Umma rulers

events.certainty_note: The Lagash–Umma conflicts are well-attested across the Early Dynastic period, but specific events cannot be assigned to individual years with confidence. This entry represents the broader pattern rather than a specific event in 2801 BCE.

events.title: Indus Valley trade networks active
events.region: Indus Valley and beyond (Mesopotamia, Persian Gulf, Central Asia)
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilisation maintains extensive trade networks with Mesopotamia, the Persian Gulf region, and Central Asia. Indus seals and artefacts appear in Mesopotamian contexts, and Mesopotamian goods reach the Indus. This trade is facilitated by maritime routes and overland caravan routes, indicating sophisticated commercial organisation.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of Indus artefacts in Mesopotamia and vice versa
Indus seals found in Mesopotamian contexts

events.certainty_note: Long-distance trade during the mature Indus phase is archaeologically well-established. The timing of specific trade goods is less certain, but the pattern is clear across the mature phase (c. 2600–1900 BCE).

disconfirming_evidence: The Lagash–Umma conflicts, while attested in inscriptions, are difficult to date precisely. Some scholars question whether all inscriptions attributed to these conflicts refer to the same historical events or whether they represent a literary/propagandistic tradition that was elaborated over time. The exact chronological sequence of Early Dynastic rulers remains contested.
historiographic_note: This year is virtually indistinguishable from -2800 in terms of available evidence. No specific events can be confidently assigned to 2801 BCE rather than adjacent years. The historical record for this period is fragmentary and depends heavily on king lists and inscriptions that may not preserve year-by-year detail. Most of the world remains unrecorded in writing.

graph_edges.note: Lagash–Umma conflicts occur during the reign of Enmetena and his predecessors
